revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1209783]] Wikidata name: shukuba [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Post stations of Japan in the Edo period}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2024}} {{italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tsumago 2009 5.JPG|thumb|{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Shukuba'''''|宿場}} were {{ill|Stage station|en|Stage station|WD=Q63987304|lt=staging post stations}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, generally located on one of the {{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}} or one of its sub-routes. They were also called ''shuku-eki'' (宿駅). These stage stations, or "{{nihongo|stage station towns|宿場町|shukuba-machi}}" developed around them, were places where travelers could rest on their journey around the nation.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C|title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan|author=William E. Deal|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2005|isbn=9780195331264|pages=61}}</ref> They were created based on policies for the transportation of goods by horseback that were developed during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian}} periods.
==History== [[File:Magome 2009 5.JPG|thumb|Nakasendō's {{ill|Magome-juku|en|Magome-juku|WD=Q1433284|lt=Magome-juku}}]] These stations were first established by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} shortly after the end of the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}. The first stations were developed along the {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} (followed by stations on the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}} and other routes). In 1601, the first of the Tōkaidō's {{ill|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q3134262|lt=fifty-three stations}} were developed, stretching from {{ill|Shinagawa-juku|en|Shinagawa-juku|WD=Q3133266|lt=Shinagawa-juku}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} to {{ill|Ōtsu-juku|en|Ōtsu-juku|WD=Q3069765|lt=Ōtsu-juku}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. Not all the post stations were built at the same time, however, as the last one was built in 1624.
The lodgings in the post stations were established for use by public officials and, when there were not enough lodgings, nearby towns were also put into use. The post station's ''[[:en:toiyaba|toiyaba]]'', ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' and sub-''honjin'' were all saved for the public officials. It was hard to receive a profit as the proprietor of these places, but the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' provided help in the form of various permits, {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}} collection and simple money lending, making it possible for the establishments to stay open. The ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'', retail stores, [[:en:Tea house|Tea house]]s, etc., which were designed for general travelers, were able to build a profit. ''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'' were intermediate post stations; though they were unofficial resting spots, they had many of the same facilities.
Generally speaking, as the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} arrived and brought along the spread of {{ill|rail transport|en|rail transport|WD=Q3565868|lt=rail transport}}, the number of travelers visiting these post stations greatly declined, as did the prosperity of the post stations.
==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.
==Preserved and rebuilt post stations== *Nationally designated Architectural Preservation Sites **{{ill|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|en|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|WD=Q10325608|lt=Aizu Nishi Kaidō}}'s {{ill|Ōuchi-juku|en|Ōuchi-juku|WD=Q3594058|lt=Ōuchi-juku}} ({{ill|Shimogō, Fukushima|en|Shimogō, Fukushima|WD=Q1205491|lt=Shimogō}}, {{ill|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|en|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|WD=Q1155802|lt=Minamiaizu District}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Hokkoku Kaidō|en|Hokkoku Kaidō|WD=Q3069767|lt=Hokkoku Kaidō}}'s Unno-juku ({{ill|Tōmi, Nagano|en|Tōmi, Nagano|WD=Q840859|lt=Tōmi}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s [[Narai-juku]] ({{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri}}, Nagano Prefecture)<ref name="kisoji">[https://web.archive.org/web/20021121124950/http://www.localinfo.nagano-idc.com/kiso/syukuba/narai/index.html Kisoji Shukuba-machi Series: Narai-juku]. {{in lang|ja}} Higashi Nihon Denshin Denwa. Accessed July 24, 2007.</ref> **Nakasendō's {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}} ([[Nagiso, Nagano|Nagiso]], Nagano Prefecture) **{{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}}'s {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=Seki-juku}} ({{ill|Kameyama, Mie|en|Kameyama, Mie|WD=Q646858|lt=Kameyama}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}})<ref name="mietourism">[http://welcome.kankomie.or.jp/english/search/spot.php?act=dtl&id=28 Mie Tourism Guide: Ancient Tokaido Seki-juku]. Mie Prefecture. Accessed November 29, 2007.</ref> **[[Saba Kaidō]]'s [[Kumagawa-shuku]] ({{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa}}, {{ill|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|en|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|WD=Q1204541|lt=Mikatakaminaka District}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Inaba Kaidō|en|Inaba Kaidō|WD=Q6013252|lt=Inaba Kaidō}}'s Hirafuku-shuku ({{ill|Sayō, Hyōgo|en|Sayō, Hyōgo|WD=Q1184480|lt=Sayō}}, {{ill|Sayō District, Hyōgo|en|Sayō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1042060|lt=Sayō District}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Ōhara-shuku ({{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|WD=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Chizu-shuku ({{ill|Chizu, Tottori|en|Chizu, Tottori|WD=Q1156212|lt=Chizu, Tottori}}, {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Yazu District}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}}) *[[Nagasaki Kaidō]]'s Koyanose-juku ({{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Uchino-shuku ({{ill|Iizuka, Fukuoka|en|Iizuka, Fukuoka|WD=Q328605|lt=Iizuka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Shiota-shuku ({{ill|Ureshino, Saga|en|Ureshino, Saga|WD=Q634130|lt=Ureshino}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}) *Tōkaidō's {{ill|Ishibe-juku|en|Ishibe-juku|WD=Q3155270|lt=Ishibe-juku}} ({{ill|Konan, Shiga|en|Konan, Shiga|WD=Q857194|lt=Konan}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}})
==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].
==See also== *''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'', rest areas along historical routes in Japan *{{ill|Caravanserai|en|Caravanserai|WD=Q186347|lt=Caravanserai}}, Middle Eastern equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *{{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}}, five major Edo period roads *''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}},'' lodgings for ''Shukuba'' travelers *''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}},'' resting area for pre-modern Japanese government officials *{{ill|Inn|en|Inn|WD=Q256020|lt=Inn}}, Western European equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}'', Edo period roads *{{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=''Ryokan''}}, Japanese rest areas

These stage stations, or "{{nihongo|stage station towns|宿場町|shukuba-machi}}" developed around them, were places where travelers could rest on their journey around the nation.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C|title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan|author=William E. Deal|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2005|isbn=9780195331264|pages=61}}</ref> They were created based on policies for the transportation of goods by horseback that were developed during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian}} periods.
==History== [[File:Magome 2009 5.JPG|thumb|Nakasendō's {{ill|Magome-juku|en|Magome-juku|WD=Q1433284|lt=Magome-juku}}]] These stations were first established by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} shortly after the end of the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}. In 1601, the first of the Tōkaidō's {{ill|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q3134262|lt=fifty-three stations}} were developed, stretching from {{ill|Shinagawa-juku|en|Shinagawa-juku|WD=Q3133266|lt=Shinagawa-juku}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} to {{ill|Ōtsu-juku|en|Ōtsu-juku|WD=Q3069765|lt=Ōtsu-juku}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. Not all the post stations were built at the same time, however, as the last one was built in 1624.
The lodgings in the post stations were established for use by public officials and, when there were not enough lodgings, nearby towns were also put into use. The post station's ''[[:en:toiyaba|toiyaba]]'', ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' and sub-''honjin'' were all saved for the public officials. It was hard to receive a profit as the proprietor of these places, but the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' provided help in the form of various permits, {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}} collection and simple money lending, making it possible for the establishments to stay open. The ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'', retail stores, [[:en:Tea house|Tea house]]s, etc., which were designed for general travelers, were able to build a profit. ''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'' were intermediate post stations; though they were unofficial resting spots, they had many of the same facilities.
Generally speaking, as the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} arrived and brought along the spread of {{ill|rail transport|en|rail transport|WD=Q3565868|lt=rail transport}}, the number of travelers visiting these post stations greatly declined, as did the prosperity of the post stations.
==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.
==Preserved and rebuilt post stations== *Nationally designated Architectural Preservation Sites **{{ill|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|en|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|WD=Q10325608|lt=Aizu Nishi Kaidō}}'s {{ill|Ōuchi-juku|en|Ōuchi-juku|WD=Q3594058|lt=Ōuchi-juku}} ({{ill|Shimogō, Fukushima|en|Shimogō, Fukushima|WD=Q1205491|lt=Shimogō}}, {{ill|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|en|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|WD=Q1155802|lt=Minamiaizu District}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Hokkoku Kaidō|en|Hokkoku Kaidō|WD=Q3069767|lt=Hokkoku Kaidō}}'s Unno-juku ({{ill|Tōmi, Nagano|en|Tōmi, Nagano|WD=Q840859|lt=Tōmi}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s [[Narai-juku]] ({{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri}}, Nagano Prefecture)<ref name="kisoji">[https://web.archive.org/web/20021121124950/http://www.localinfo.nagano-idc.com/kiso/syukuba/narai/index.html Kisoji Shukuba-machi Series: Narai-juku]. {{in lang|ja}} Higashi Nihon Denshin Denwa. Accessed July 24, 2007.</ref> **Nakasendō's {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}} ([[Nagiso, Nagano|Nagiso]], Nagano Prefecture) **{{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}}'s {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=Seki-juku}} ({{ill|Kameyama, Mie|en|Kameyama, Mie|WD=Q646858|lt=Kameyama}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}})<ref name="mietourism">[http://welcome.kankomie.or.jp/english/search/spot.php?act=dtl&id=28 Mie Tourism Guide: Ancient Tokaido Seki-juku]. Mie Prefecture. Accessed November 29, 2007.</ref> **[[Saba Kaidō]]'s [[Kumagawa-shuku]] ({{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa}}, {{ill|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|en|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|WD=Q1204541|lt=Mikatakaminaka District}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Inaba Kaidō|en|Inaba Kaidō|WD=Q6013252|lt=Inaba Kaidō}}'s Hirafuku-shuku ({{ill|Sayō, Hyōgo|en|Sayō, Hyōgo|WD=Q1184480|lt=Sayō}}, {{ill|Sayō District, Hyōgo|en|Sayō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1042060|lt=Sayō District}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Ōhara-shuku ({{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|WD=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Chizu-shuku ({{ill|Chizu, Tottori|en|Chizu, Tottori|WD=Q1156212|lt=Chizu, Tottori}}, {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Yazu District}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}}) *[[Nagasaki Kaidō]]'s Koyanose-juku ({{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Uchino-shuku ({{ill|Iizuka, Fukuoka|en|Iizuka, Fukuoka|WD=Q328605|lt=Iizuka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Shiota-shuku ({{ill|Ureshino, Saga|en|Ureshino, Saga|WD=Q634130|lt=Ureshino}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}) *Tōkaidō's {{ill|Ishibe-juku|en|Ishibe-juku|WD=Q3155270|lt=Ishibe-juku}} ({{ill|Konan, Shiga|en|Konan, Shiga|WD=Q857194|lt=Konan}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}})
==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].
==See also== *''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'', rest areas along historical routes in Japan *{{ill|Caravanserai|en|Caravanserai|WD=Q186347|lt=Caravanserai}}, Middle Eastern equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *{{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}}, five major Edo period roads *''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}},'' lodgings for ''Shukuba'' travelers *''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}},'' resting area for pre-modern Japanese government officials *{{ill|Inn|en|Inn|WD=Q256020|lt=Inn}}, Western European equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}'', Edo period roads *{{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=''Ryokan''}}, Japanese rest areas

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1209783]] Wikidata name: shukuba [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Post stations of Japan in the Edo period}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2024}} {{italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tsumago 2009 5.JPG|thumb|{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Shukuba'''''|宿場}} were {{ill|Stage station|en|Stage station|WD=Q63987304|lt=staging post stations}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, generally located on one of the {{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}} or one of its sub-routes. They were also called ''shuku-eki'' (宿駅). These stage stations, or "{{nihongo|stage station towns|宿場町|shukuba-machi}}" developed around them, were places where travelers could rest on their journey around the nation.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C|title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan|author=William E. Deal|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2005|isbn=9780195331264|pages=61}}</ref> They were created based on policies for the transportation of goods by horseback that were developed during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian}} periods.
==History== [[File:Magome 2009 5.JPG|thumb|Nakasendō's {{ill|Magome-juku|en|Magome-juku|WD=Q1433284|lt=Magome-juku}}]] These stations were first established by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} shortly after the end of the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}. The first stations were developed along the {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} (followed by stations on the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}} and other routes). In 1601, the first of the Tōkaidō's {{ill|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q3134262|lt=fifty-three stations}} were developed, stretching from {{ill|Shinagawa-juku|en|Shinagawa-juku|WD=Q3133266|lt=Shinagawa-juku}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} to {{ill|Ōtsu-juku|en|Ōtsu-juku|WD=Q3069765|lt=Ōtsu-juku}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. Not all the post stations were built at the same time, however, as the last one was built in 1624.
The lodgings in the post stations were established for use by public officials and, when there were not enough lodgings, nearby towns were also put into use. The post station's ''[[:en:toiyaba|toiyaba]]'', ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' and sub-''honjin'' were all saved for the public officials. It was hard to receive a profit as the proprietor of these places, but the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' provided help in the form of various permits, {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}} collection and simple money lending, making it possible for the establishments to stay open. The ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'', retail stores, [[:en:Tea house|Tea house]]s, etc., which were designed for general travelers, were able to build a profit. ''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'' were intermediate post stations; though they were unofficial resting spots, they had many of the same facilities.
Generally speaking, as the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} arrived and brought along the spread of {{ill|rail transport|en|rail transport|WD=Q3565868|lt=rail transport}}, the number of travelers visiting these post stations greatly declined, as did the prosperity of the post stations.
==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.
==Preserved and rebuilt post stations== *Nationally designated Architectural Preservation Sites **{{ill|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|en|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|WD=Q10325608|lt=Aizu Nishi Kaidō}}'s {{ill|Ōuchi-juku|en|Ōuchi-juku|WD=Q3594058|lt=Ōuchi-juku}} ({{ill|Shimogō, Fukushima|en|Shimogō, Fukushima|WD=Q1205491|lt=Shimogō}}, {{ill|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|en|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|WD=Q1155802|lt=Minamiaizu District}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Hokkoku Kaidō|en|Hokkoku Kaidō|WD=Q3069767|lt=Hokkoku Kaidō}}'s Unno-juku ({{ill|Tōmi, Nagano|en|Tōmi, Nagano|WD=Q840859|lt=Tōmi}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s [[Narai-juku]] ({{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri}}, Nagano Prefecture)<ref name="kisoji">[https://web.archive.org/web/20021121124950/http://www.localinfo.nagano-idc.com/kiso/syukuba/narai/index.html Kisoji Shukuba-machi Series: Narai-juku]. {{in lang|ja}} Higashi Nihon Denshin Denwa. Accessed July 24, 2007.</ref> **Nakasendō's {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}} ([[Nagiso, Nagano|Nagiso]], Nagano Prefecture) **{{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}}'s {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=Seki-juku}} ({{ill|Kameyama, Mie|en|Kameyama, Mie|WD=Q646858|lt=Kameyama}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}})<ref name="mietourism">[http://welcome.kankomie.or.jp/english/search/spot.php?act=dtl&id=28 Mie Tourism Guide: Ancient Tokaido Seki-juku]. Mie Prefecture. Accessed November 29, 2007.</ref> **[[Saba Kaidō]]'s [[Kumagawa-shuku]] ({{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa}}, {{ill|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|en|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|WD=Q1204541|lt=Mikatakaminaka District}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Inaba Kaidō|en|Inaba Kaidō|WD=Q6013252|lt=Inaba Kaidō}}'s Hirafuku-shuku ({{ill|Sayō, Hyōgo|en|Sayō, Hyōgo|WD=Q1184480|lt=Sayō}}, {{ill|Sayō District, Hyōgo|en|Sayō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1042060|lt=Sayō District}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Ōhara-shuku ({{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|WD=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Chizu-shuku ({{ill|Chizu, Tottori|en|Chizu, Tottori|WD=Q1156212|lt=Chizu, Tottori}}, {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Yazu District}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}}) *[[Nagasaki Kaidō]]'s Koyanose-juku ({{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Uchino-shuku ({{ill|Iizuka, Fukuoka|en|Iizuka, Fukuoka|WD=Q328605|lt=Iizuka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Shiota-shuku ({{ill|Ureshino, Saga|en|Ureshino, Saga|WD=Q634130|lt=Ureshino}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}) *Tōkaidō's {{ill|Ishibe-juku|en|Ishibe-juku|WD=Q3155270|lt=Ishibe-juku}} ({{ill|Konan, Shiga|en|Konan, Shiga|WD=Q857194|lt=Konan}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}})
==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]]
{{Short description|Post stations of Japan in the Edo period}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2024}} {{italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tsumago 2009 5.JPG|thumb|{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Shukuba'''''|宿場}} were {{ill|Stage station|en|Stage station|WD=Q63987304|lt=staging post stations}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, generally located on one of the {{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}} or one of its sub-routes. They were also called ''shuku-eki'' (宿駅). These stage stations, or "{{nihongo|stage station towns|宿場町|shukuba-machi}}" developed around them, were places where travelers could rest on their journey around the nation.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C|title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan|author=William E. Deal|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2005|isbn=9780195331264|pages=61}}</ref> They were created based on policies for the transportation of goods by horseback that were developed during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian}} periods.
==History== [[File:Magome 2009 5.JPG|thumb|Nakasendō's {{ill|Magome-juku|en|Magome-juku|WD=Q1433284|lt=Magome-juku}}]] These stations were first established by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} shortly after the end of the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}. The first stations were developed along the {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} (followed by stations on the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}} and other routes). In 1601, the first of the Tōkaidō's {{ill|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q3134262|lt=fifty-three stations}} were developed, stretching from {{ill|Shinagawa-juku|en|Shinagawa-juku|WD=Q3133266|lt=Shinagawa-juku}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} to {{ill|Ōtsu-juku|en|Ōtsu-juku|WD=Q3069765|lt=Ōtsu-juku}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. Not all the post stations were built at the same time, however, as the last one was built in 1624.
The lodgings in the post stations were established for use by public officials and, when there were not enough lodgings, nearby towns were also put into use. The post station's ''[[:en:toiyaba|toiyaba]]'', ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' and sub-''honjin'' were all saved for the public officials. It was hard to receive a profit as the proprietor of these places, but the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' provided help in the form of various permits, {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}} collection and simple money lending, making it possible for the establishments to stay open. The ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'', retail stores, [[:en:Tea house|Tea house]]s, etc., which were designed for general travelers, were able to build a profit. ''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'' were intermediate post stations; though they were unofficial resting spots, they had many of the same facilities.
Generally speaking, as the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} arrived and brought along the spread of {{ill|rail transport|en|rail transport|WD=Q3565868|lt=rail transport}}, the number of travelers visiting these post stations greatly declined, as did the prosperity of the post stations.
==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.
==Preserved and rebuilt post stations== *Nationally designated Architectural Preservation Sites **{{ill|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|en|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|WD=Q10325608|lt=Aizu Nishi Kaidō}}'s {{ill|Ōuchi-juku|en|Ōuchi-juku|WD=Q3594058|lt=Ōuchi-juku}} ({{ill|Shimogō, Fukushima|en|Shimogō, Fukushima|WD=Q1205491|lt=Shimogō}}, {{ill|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|en|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|WD=Q1155802|lt=Minamiaizu District}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Hokkoku Kaidō|en|Hokkoku Kaidō|WD=Q3069767|lt=Hokkoku Kaidō}}'s Unno-juku ({{ill|Tōmi, Nagano|en|Tōmi, Nagano|WD=Q840859|lt=Tōmi}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s [[Narai-juku]] ({{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri}}, Nagano Prefecture)<ref name="kisoji">[https://web.archive.org/web/20021121124950/http://www.localinfo.nagano-idc.com/kiso/syukuba/narai/index.html Kisoji Shukuba-machi Series: Narai-juku]. {{in lang|ja}} Higashi Nihon Denshin Denwa. Accessed July 24, 2007.</ref> **Nakasendō's {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}} ([[Nagiso, Nagano|Nagiso]], Nagano Prefecture) **{{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}}'s {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=Seki-juku}} ({{ill|Kameyama, Mie|en|Kameyama, Mie|WD=Q646858|lt=Kameyama}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}})<ref name="mietourism">[http://welcome.kankomie.or.jp/english/search/spot.php?act=dtl&id=28 Mie Tourism Guide: Ancient Tokaido Seki-juku]. Mie Prefecture. Accessed November 29, 2007.</ref> **[[Saba Kaidō]]'s [[Kumagawa-shuku]] ({{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa}}, {{ill|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|en|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|WD=Q1204541|lt=Mikatakaminaka District}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Inaba Kaidō|en|Inaba Kaidō|WD=Q6013252|lt=Inaba Kaidō}}'s Hirafuku-shuku ({{ill|Sayō, Hyōgo|en|Sayō, Hyōgo|WD=Q1184480|lt=Sayō}}, {{ill|Sayō District, Hyōgo|en|Sayō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1042060|lt=Sayō District}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Ōhara-shuku ({{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|WD=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Chizu-shuku ({{ill|Chizu, Tottori|en|Chizu, Tottori|WD=Q1156212|lt=Chizu, Tottori}}, {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Yazu District}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}}) *[[Nagasaki Kaidō]]'s Koyanose-juku ({{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Uchino-shuku ({{ill|Iizuka, Fukuoka|en|Iizuka, Fukuoka|WD=Q328605|lt=Iizuka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Shiota-shuku ({{ill|Ureshino, Saga|en|Ureshino, Saga|WD=Q634130|lt=Ureshino}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}) *Tōkaidō's {{ill|Ishibe-juku|en|Ishibe-juku|WD=Q3155270|lt=Ishibe-juku}} ({{ill|Konan, Shiga|en|Konan, Shiga|WD=Q857194|lt=Konan}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}})
==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].
==See also== *''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'', rest areas along historical routes in Japan *{{ill|Caravanserai|en|Caravanserai|WD=Q186347|lt=Caravanserai}}, Middle Eastern equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *{{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}}, five major Edo period roads *''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}},'' lodgings for ''Shukuba'' travelers *''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}},'' resting area for pre-modern Japanese government officials *{{ill|Inn|en|Inn|WD=Q256020|lt=Inn}}, Western European equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}'', Edo period roads *{{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=''Ryokan''}}, Japanese rest areas

{{Short description|Post stations of Japan in the Edo period}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2024}} {{italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tsumago 2009 5.JPG|thumb|{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Shukuba'''''|宿場}} were {{ill|Stage station|en|Stage station|WD=Q63987304|lt=staging post stations}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, generally located on one of the {{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}} or one of its sub-routes. They were also called ''shuku-eki'' (宿駅). These stage stations, or "{{nihongo|stage station towns|宿場町|shukuba-machi}}" developed around them, were places where travelers could rest on their journey around the nation.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C|title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan|author=William E. Deal|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2005|isbn=9780195331264|pages=61}}</ref> They were created based on policies for the transportation of goods by horseback that were developed during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian}} periods.
==History== [[File:Magome 2009 5.JPG|thumb|Nakasendō's {{ill|Magome-juku|en|Magome-juku|WD=Q1433284|lt=Magome-juku}}]] These stations were first established by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} shortly after the end of the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}. The first stations were developed along the {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}} (followed by stations on the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}} and other routes). In 1601, the first of the Tōkaidō's {{ill|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q3134262|lt=fifty-three stations}} were developed, stretching from {{ill|Shinagawa-juku|en|Shinagawa-juku|WD=Q3133266|lt=Shinagawa-juku}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} to {{ill|Ōtsu-juku|en|Ōtsu-juku|WD=Q3069765|lt=Ōtsu-juku}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. Not all the post stations were built at the same time, however, as the last one was built in 1624.
The lodgings in the post stations were established for use by public officials and, when there were not enough lodgings, nearby towns were also put into use. The post station's ''[[:en:toiyaba|toiyaba]]'', ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' and sub-''honjin'' were all saved for the public officials. It was hard to receive a profit as the proprietor of these places, but the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' provided help in the form of various permits, {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}} collection and simple money lending, making it possible for the establishments to stay open. The ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'', retail stores, [[:en:Tea house|Tea house]]s, etc., which were designed for general travelers, were able to build a profit. ''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'' were intermediate post stations; though they were unofficial resting spots, they had many of the same facilities.
Generally speaking, as the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} arrived and brought along the spread of {{ill|rail transport|en|rail transport|WD=Q3565868|lt=rail transport}}, the number of travelers visiting these post stations greatly declined, as did the prosperity of the post stations.
==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.
==Preserved and rebuilt post stations== *Nationally designated Architectural Preservation Sites **{{ill|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|en|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|WD=Q10325608|lt=Aizu Nishi Kaidō}}'s {{ill|Ōuchi-juku|en|Ōuchi-juku|WD=Q3594058|lt=Ōuchi-juku}} ({{ill|Shimogō, Fukushima|en|Shimogō, Fukushima|WD=Q1205491|lt=Shimogō}}, {{ill|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|en|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|WD=Q1155802|lt=Minamiaizu District}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Hokkoku Kaidō|en|Hokkoku Kaidō|WD=Q3069767|lt=Hokkoku Kaidō}}'s Unno-juku ({{ill|Tōmi, Nagano|en|Tōmi, Nagano|WD=Q840859|lt=Tōmi}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō}}'s [[Narai-juku]] ({{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri}}, Nagano Prefecture)<ref name="kisoji">[https://web.archive.org/web/20021121124950/http://www.localinfo.nagano-idc.com/kiso/syukuba/narai/index.html Kisoji Shukuba-machi Series: Narai-juku]. {{in lang|ja}} Higashi Nihon Denshin Denwa. Accessed July 24, 2007.</ref> **Nakasendō's {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku}} ([[Nagiso, Nagano|Nagiso]], Nagano Prefecture) **{{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō}}'s {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=Seki-juku}} ({{ill|Kameyama, Mie|en|Kameyama, Mie|WD=Q646858|lt=Kameyama}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}})<ref name="mietourism">[http://welcome.kankomie.or.jp/english/search/spot.php?act=dtl&id=28 Mie Tourism Guide: Ancient Tokaido Seki-juku]. Mie Prefecture. Accessed November 29, 2007.</ref> **[[Saba Kaidō]]'s [[Kumagawa-shuku]] ({{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa}}, {{ill|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|en|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|WD=Q1204541|lt=Mikatakaminaka District}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Inaba Kaidō|en|Inaba Kaidō|WD=Q6013252|lt=Inaba Kaidō}}'s Hirafuku-shuku ({{ill|Sayō, Hyōgo|en|Sayō, Hyōgo|WD=Q1184480|lt=Sayō}}, {{ill|Sayō District, Hyōgo|en|Sayō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1042060|lt=Sayō District}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Ōhara-shuku ({{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|WD=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Chizu-shuku ({{ill|Chizu, Tottori|en|Chizu, Tottori|WD=Q1156212|lt=Chizu, Tottori}}, {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Yazu District}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture}}) *[[Nagasaki Kaidō]]'s Koyanose-juku ({{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Uchino-shuku ({{ill|Iizuka, Fukuoka|en|Iizuka, Fukuoka|WD=Q328605|lt=Iizuka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Shiota-shuku ({{ill|Ureshino, Saga|en|Ureshino, Saga|WD=Q634130|lt=Ureshino}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}) *Tōkaidō's {{ill|Ishibe-juku|en|Ishibe-juku|WD=Q3155270|lt=Ishibe-juku}} ({{ill|Konan, Shiga|en|Konan, Shiga|WD=Q857194|lt=Konan}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}})
==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].
==See also== *''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku}}'', rest areas along historical routes in Japan *{{ill|Caravanserai|en|Caravanserai|WD=Q186347|lt=Caravanserai}}, Middle Eastern equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *{{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes}}, five major Edo period roads *''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago}},'' lodgings for ''Shukuba'' travelers *''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin}},'' resting area for pre-modern Japanese government officials *{{ill|Inn|en|Inn|WD=Q256020|lt=Inn}}, Western European equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}'', Edo period roads *{{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=''Ryokan''}}, Japanese rest areas
==References== {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]]
{{Short description|Post stations of Japan in the Edo period}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2024}} {{italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tsumago 2009 5.JPG|thumb|{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}}'s {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku|1=Tsumago-juku}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Shukuba'''''|宿場}} were {{ill|Stage station|en|Stage station|WD=Q63987304|lt=staging post stations|1=Stage station}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, generally located on one of the {{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes|1=Edo Five Routes}} or one of its sub-routes. They were also called ''shuku-eki'' (宿駅). These stage stations, or "{{nihongo|stage station towns|宿場町|shukuba-machi}}" developed around them, were places where travelers could rest on their journey around the nation.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C|title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan|author=William E. Deal|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2005|isbn=9780195331264|pages=61}}</ref> They were created based on policies for the transportation of goods by horseback that were developed during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} periods.
==History== [[File:Magome 2009 5.JPG|thumb|Nakasendō's {{ill|Magome-juku|en|Magome-juku|WD=Q1433284|lt=Magome-juku|1=Magome-juku}}]] These stations were first established by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} shortly after the end of the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. The first stations were developed along the {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}} (followed by stations on the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}} and other routes). In 1601, the first of the Tōkaidō's {{ill|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q3134262|lt=fifty-three stations|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}} were developed, stretching from {{ill|Shinagawa-juku|en|Shinagawa-juku|WD=Q3133266|lt=Shinagawa-juku|1=Shinagawa-juku}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} to {{ill|Ōtsu-juku|en|Ōtsu-juku|WD=Q3069765|lt=Ōtsu-juku|1=Ōtsu-juku}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. Not all the post stations were built at the same time, however, as the last one was built in 1624.
The lodgings in the post stations were established for use by public officials and, when there were not enough lodgings, nearby towns were also put into use. The post station's ''[[:en:toiyaba|toiyaba]]'', ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin|1=Honjin}}'' and sub-''honjin'' were all saved for the public officials. It was hard to receive a profit as the proprietor of these places, but the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' provided help in the form of various permits, {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}} collection and simple money lending, making it possible for the establishments to stay open. The ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago|1=Hatago}}'', retail stores, [[:en:Tea house|Tea house]]s, etc., which were designed for general travelers, were able to build a profit. ''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku|1=Ai no shuku}}'' were intermediate post stations; though they were unofficial resting spots, they had many of the same facilities.
Generally speaking, as the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} arrived and brought along the spread of {{ill|rail transport|en|rail transport|WD=Q3565868|lt=rail transport|1=Rail transport}}, the number of travelers visiting these post stations greatly declined, as did the prosperity of the post stations.
==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku|1=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku|1=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku|1=Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin|1=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago|1=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya|1=Ochaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu|1=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.
==Preserved and rebuilt post stations== *Nationally designated Architectural Preservation Sites **{{ill|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|en|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|WD=Q10325608|lt=Aizu Nishi Kaidō|1=Aizu Nishi Kaidō}}'s {{ill|Ōuchi-juku|en|Ōuchi-juku|WD=Q3594058|lt=Ōuchi-juku|1=Ōuchi-juku}} ({{ill|Shimogō, Fukushima|en|Shimogō, Fukushima|WD=Q1205491|lt=Shimogō|1=Shimogō, Fukushima}}, {{ill|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|en|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|WD=Q1155802|lt=Minamiaizu District|1=Minamiaizu District, Fukushima}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Hokkoku Kaidō|en|Hokkoku Kaidō|WD=Q3069767|lt=Hokkoku Kaidō|1=Hokkoku Kaidō}}'s Unno-juku ({{ill|Tōmi, Nagano|en|Tōmi, Nagano|WD=Q840859|lt=Tōmi|1=Tōmi, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}}'s [[Narai-juku]] ({{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri|1=Shiojiri, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture)<ref name="kisoji">[https://web.archive.org/web/20021121124950/http://www.localinfo.nagano-idc.com/kiso/syukuba/narai/index.html Kisoji Shukuba-machi Series: Narai-juku]. {{in lang|ja}} Higashi Nihon Denshin Denwa. Accessed July 24, 2007.</ref> **Nakasendō's {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku|1=Tsumago-juku}} ([[Nagiso, Nagano|Nagiso]], Nagano Prefecture) **{{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}}'s {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=Seki-juku|1=Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)}} ({{ill|Kameyama, Mie|en|Kameyama, Mie|WD=Q646858|lt=Kameyama|1=Kameyama, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}})<ref name="mietourism">[http://welcome.kankomie.or.jp/english/search/spot.php?act=dtl&id=28 Mie Tourism Guide: Ancient Tokaido Seki-juku]. Mie Prefecture. Accessed November 29, 2007.</ref> **[[Saba Kaidō]]'s [[Kumagawa-shuku]] ({{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa|1=Wakasa, Fukui}}, {{ill|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|en|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|WD=Q1204541|lt=Mikatakaminaka District|1=Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Inaba Kaidō|en|Inaba Kaidō|WD=Q6013252|lt=Inaba Kaidō|1=Inaba Kaidō}}'s Hirafuku-shuku ({{ill|Sayō, Hyōgo|en|Sayō, Hyōgo|WD=Q1184480|lt=Sayō|1=Sayō, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Sayō District, Hyōgo|en|Sayō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1042060|lt=Sayō District|1=Sayō District, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Ōhara-shuku ({{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|WD=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka|1=Mimasaka, Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Chizu-shuku ({{ill|Chizu, Tottori|en|Chizu, Tottori|WD=Q1156212|lt=Chizu, Tottori|1=Chizu, Tottori}}, {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Yazu District|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}) *[[Nagasaki Kaidō]]'s Koyanose-juku ({{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|1=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Uchino-shuku ({{ill|Iizuka, Fukuoka|en|Iizuka, Fukuoka|WD=Q328605|lt=Iizuka|1=Iizuka, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Shiota-shuku ({{ill|Ureshino, Saga|en|Ureshino, Saga|WD=Q634130|lt=Ureshino|1=Ureshino, Saga}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}) *Tōkaidō's {{ill|Ishibe-juku|en|Ishibe-juku|WD=Q3155270|lt=Ishibe-juku|1=Ishibe-juku}} ({{ill|Konan, Shiga|en|Konan, Shiga|WD=Q857194|lt=Konan|1=Konan, Shiga}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}})
==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki|1=Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|1=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].
==See also== *''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku|1=Ai no shuku}}'', rest areas along historical routes in Japan *{{ill|Caravanserai|en|Caravanserai|WD=Q186347|lt=Caravanserai|1=Caravanserai}}, Middle Eastern equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *{{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes|1=Edo Five Routes}}, five major Edo period roads *''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago|1=Hatago}},'' lodgings for ''Shukuba'' travelers *''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin|1=Honjin}},'' resting area for pre-modern Japanese government officials *{{ill|Inn|en|Inn|WD=Q256020|lt=Inn|1=Inn}}, Western European equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō|1=Kaidō}}'', Edo period roads *{{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=''Ryokan''|1=Ryokan}}, Japanese rest areas

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[el:Σουκούμπα]][[en:Shukuba]][[fa:شوکوبا]][[fi:Shukuba]][[fr:Shukuba]][[hu:Sukuba]][[ja:宿場]][[ko:슈쿠바]][[no:Shukuba]][[pl:Shukuba]][[ru:Сюкуба]][[su:Shukuba]][[sv:Shukuba]][[tr:Shukuba]][[uk:Станція (Японія)]][[zh:宿場]]
{{Short description|Post stations of Japan in the Edo period}} {{Refimprove|date=August 2024}} {{italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tsumago 2009 5.JPG|thumb|{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}}'s {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku|1=Tsumago-juku}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Shukuba'''''|宿場}} were {{ill|Stage station|en|Stage station|WD=Q63987304|lt=staging post stations|1=Stage station}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, generally located on one of the {{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes|1=Edo Five Routes}} or one of its sub-routes. They were also called ''shuku-eki'' (宿駅). These stage stations, or "{{nihongo|stage station towns|宿場町|shukuba-machi}}" developed around them, were places where travelers could rest on their journey around the nation.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=i0ni1NmbYe0C|title=Handbook to Life in Medieval and Early Modern Japan|author=William E. Deal|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2005|isbn=9780195331264|pages=61}}</ref> They were created based on policies for the transportation of goods by horseback that were developed during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara|1=Nara period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} periods.
==History== [[File:Magome 2009 5.JPG|thumb|Nakasendō's {{ill|Magome-juku|en|Magome-juku|WD=Q1433284|lt=Magome-juku|1=Magome-juku}}]] These stations were first established by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} shortly after the end of the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. The first stations were developed along the {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}} (followed by stations on the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}} and other routes). In 1601, the first of the Tōkaidō's {{ill|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|53 Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q3134262|lt=fifty-three stations|1=53 Stations of the Tōkaidō}} were developed, stretching from {{ill|Shinagawa-juku|en|Shinagawa-juku|WD=Q3133266|lt=Shinagawa-juku|1=Shinagawa-juku}} in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} to {{ill|Ōtsu-juku|en|Ōtsu-juku|WD=Q3069765|lt=Ōtsu-juku|1=Ōtsu-juku}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. Not all the post stations were built at the same time, however, as the last one was built in 1624.
The lodgings in the post stations were established for use by public officials and, when there were not enough lodgings, nearby towns were also put into use. The post station's ''[[:en:toiyaba|toiyaba]]'', ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin|1=Honjin}}'' and sub-''honjin'' were all saved for the public officials. It was hard to receive a profit as the proprietor of these places, but the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' provided help in the form of various permits, {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}} collection and simple money lending, making it possible for the establishments to stay open. The ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago|1=Hatago}}'', retail stores, [[:en:Tea house|Tea house]]s, etc., which were designed for general travelers, were able to build a profit. ''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku|1=Ai no shuku}}'' were intermediate post stations; though they were unofficial resting spots, they had many of the same facilities.
Generally speaking, as the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} arrived and brought along the spread of {{ill|rail transport|en|rail transport|WD=Q3565868|lt=rail transport|1=Rail transport}}, the number of travelers visiting these post stations greatly declined, as did the prosperity of the post stations.
==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku|1=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku|1=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku|1=Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin|1=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago|1=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya|1=Ochaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu|1=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.
==Preserved and rebuilt post stations== *Nationally designated Architectural Preservation Sites **{{ill|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|en|Aizu Nishi Kaidō|WD=Q10325608|lt=Aizu Nishi Kaidō|1=Aizu Nishi Kaidō}}'s {{ill|Ōuchi-juku|en|Ōuchi-juku|WD=Q3594058|lt=Ōuchi-juku|1=Ōuchi-juku}} ({{ill|Shimogō, Fukushima|en|Shimogō, Fukushima|WD=Q1205491|lt=Shimogō|1=Shimogō, Fukushima}}, {{ill|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|en|Minamiaizu District, Fukushima|WD=Q1155802|lt=Minamiaizu District|1=Minamiaizu District, Fukushima}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Hokkoku Kaidō|en|Hokkoku Kaidō|WD=Q3069767|lt=Hokkoku Kaidō|1=Hokkoku Kaidō}}'s Unno-juku ({{ill|Tōmi, Nagano|en|Tōmi, Nagano|WD=Q840859|lt=Tōmi|1=Tōmi, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}) **{{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|WD=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}}'s [[Narai-juku]] ({{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri|1=Shiojiri, Nagano}}, Nagano Prefecture)<ref name="kisoji">[https://web.archive.org/web/20021121124950/http://www.localinfo.nagano-idc.com/kiso/syukuba/narai/index.html Kisoji Shukuba-machi Series: Narai-juku]. {{in lang|ja}} Higashi Nihon Denshin Denwa. Accessed July 24, 2007.</ref> **Nakasendō's {{ill|Tsumago-juku|en|Tsumago-juku|WD=Q1079904|lt=Tsumago-juku|1=Tsumago-juku}} ([[Nagiso, Nagano|Nagiso]], Nagano Prefecture) **{{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|WD=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}}'s {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=Seki-juku|1=Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)}} ({{ill|Kameyama, Mie|en|Kameyama, Mie|WD=Q646858|lt=Kameyama|1=Kameyama, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}})<ref name="mietourism">[http://welcome.kankomie.or.jp/english/search/spot.php?act=dtl&id=28 Mie Tourism Guide: Ancient Tokaido Seki-juku]. Mie Prefecture. Accessed November 29, 2007.</ref> **[[Saba Kaidō]]'s [[Kumagawa-shuku]] ({{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa|1=Wakasa, Fukui}}, {{ill|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|en|Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui|WD=Q1204541|lt=Mikatakaminaka District|1=Mikatakaminaka District, Fukui}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}}) *{{ill|Inaba Kaidō|en|Inaba Kaidō|WD=Q6013252|lt=Inaba Kaidō|1=Inaba Kaidō}}'s Hirafuku-shuku ({{ill|Sayō, Hyōgo|en|Sayō, Hyōgo|WD=Q1184480|lt=Sayō|1=Sayō, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Sayō District, Hyōgo|en|Sayō District, Hyōgo|WD=Q1042060|lt=Sayō District|1=Sayō District, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Ōhara-shuku ({{ill|Mimasaka, Okayama|en|Mimasaka, Okayama|WD=Q861279|lt=Mimasaka|1=Mimasaka, Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}) *Inaba Kaidō's Chizu-shuku ({{ill|Chizu, Tottori|en|Chizu, Tottori|WD=Q1156212|lt=Chizu, Tottori|1=Chizu, Tottori}}, {{ill|Yazu District, Tottori|en|Yazu District, Tottori|WD=Q1043163|lt=Yazu District|1=Yazu District, Tottori}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}) *[[Nagasaki Kaidō]]'s Koyanose-juku ({{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|1=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Uchino-shuku ({{ill|Iizuka, Fukuoka|en|Iizuka, Fukuoka|WD=Q328605|lt=Iizuka|1=Iizuka, Fukuoka}}, Fukuoka Prefecture) *Nagasaki Kaidō's Shiota-shuku ({{ill|Ureshino, Saga|en|Ureshino, Saga|WD=Q634130|lt=Ureshino|1=Ureshino, Saga}}, {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}) *Tōkaidō's {{ill|Ishibe-juku|en|Ishibe-juku|WD=Q3155270|lt=Ishibe-juku|1=Ishibe-juku}} ({{ill|Konan, Shiga|en|Konan, Shiga|WD=Q857194|lt=Konan|1=Konan, Shiga}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}})
==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki|1=Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|1=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].
==See also== *''{{ill|Ai no shuku|en|Ai no shuku|WD=Q2827758|lt=Ai no shuku|1=Ai no shuku}}'', rest areas along historical routes in Japan *{{ill|Caravanserai|en|Caravanserai|WD=Q186347|lt=Caravanserai|1=Caravanserai}}, Middle Eastern equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *{{ill|Edo Five Routes|en|Edo Five Routes|WD=Q1195921|lt=Edo Five Routes|1=Edo Five Routes}}, five major Edo period roads *''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago|1=Hatago}},'' lodgings for ''Shukuba'' travelers *''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin|1=Honjin}},'' resting area for pre-modern Japanese government officials *{{ill|Inn|en|Inn|WD=Q256020|lt=Inn|1=Inn}}, Western European equivalent of ''Shukuba'' *''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō|1=Kaidō}}'', Edo period roads *{{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=''Ryokan''|1=Ryokan}}, Japanese rest areas

==Post station facilities== [[File:SPHFM04s3200.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Samegai-juku|en|Samegai-juku|WD=Q3470759|lt=Samegai-juku|1=Samegai-juku}}'s ''toiyaba'']] [[File:Kusatsujuku-honjin Kusatsu Shiga Pref01s3s4500.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kusatsu-juku|en|Kusatsu-juku|WD=Q3200642|lt=Kusatsu-juku|1=Kusatsu-juku}}'s ''honjin'']] [[File:Oohasiya Inn.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q2828947|lt=Akasaka-juku|1=Akasaka-juku (Tōkaidō)}}'s ''hatago'']] [[File:Hirafuku kawabata02s2400.jpg|thumb|Hirafuku-shuku's ''Kawabata'']]
* ''[[:en:Tonya (Japan)|Ton'yaba]]'' (問屋場): General offices that helped manage the post town. * ''{{ill|Honjin|en|Honjin|WD=Q994817|lt=Honjin|1=Honjin}}'' (本陣): Rest areas and lodgings built for use by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and court nobles. ''Honjin'' were not businesses; instead, large residences in the post towns were often designated as lodging for government officials. * ''Waki-honjin'' (脇本陣): These facilities were also for use by samurai and court nobles, but general travelers could also stay here if there were vacancies. * ''{{ill|Hatago|en|Hatago|WD=Q1049388|lt=Hatago|1=Hatago}}'' (旅籠): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers and also served food. * ''[[Kichin-yado]]'' (木賃宿): Facilities that offered accommodations to general travelers, but did not serve food. * ''{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=Chaya|1=Ochaya}}'' (茶屋): Rest areas that sold tea, food and alcohol to travelers. * Shops: General shops built to sell wares to travelers. * ''{{ill|Kōsatsu|en|Kōsatsu|WD=Q11670187|lt=Kōsatsu|1=Kōsatsu}}'' (高札): Signboards on which the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'s proclamations were posted.

==Cultural heritage== [[File:Hiroshige48 seki.jpg|thumb|220x220px|The 47th Station: Early departure from the inn at the {{ill|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|en|Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)|WD=Q3033351|lt=shukuba of Seki|1=Seki-juku (Tōkaidō)}}]] Many shukuba are preserved as cultural heritage. They are also often the subjects of [[Ukiyo-e]], such as in ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|WD=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|1=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' by [[Hiroshige]].
